inline_review:
commit 1e3f82ec25423c3e5a400a2068002129f04c7668
Author: Marek Szyprowski <m.szyprowski@samsung.com>

of: reserved_mem: rework fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()

This commit moves the content of fdt_reserved_mem_save_node() into
fdt_init_reserved_mem_node() to perform the initialization of the reserved
memory region in a single step instead of two.

> diff --git a/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c b/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c
> index 229573d6dacb..7146c1ba6ae1 100644
> --- a/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c
> +++ b/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c
> @@ -102,36 +102,13 @@ static void __init alloc_reserved_mem_array(void)
>  	reserved_mem = new_array;
>  }
>  
> -static void __init fdt_init_reserved_mem_node(struct reserved_mem *rmem,
> -					      unsigned long node);
> +static void fdt_init_reserved_mem_node(unsigned long node, const char *uname,
> +				       phys_addr_t base, phys_addr_t size);

Is there a missing __init annotation here? The actual function definition
below is explicitly marked with __init, so this might cause section mismatch
or missing prototype warnings during the build.

[ ... ]

> @@ -590,21 +568,31 @@ static int __init __reserved_mem_init_node(struct reserved_mem *rmem,
>  	return ret;
>  }
>  
> -
>  /**
>   * fdt_init_reserved_mem_node() - Initialize a reserved memory region
> - * @rmem: reserved_mem struct of the memory region to be initialized.
>   * @node: fdt node of the initialized region
>   *
>   * This function is used to call the region specific initialization
> - * function for a reserved memory region.
> + * function for a reserved memory region save all region specific data to
> + * reserved_mem array to let of_reserved_mem_lookup() function find it.
>   */
> -static void __init fdt_init_reserved_mem_node(struct reserved_mem *rmem,
> -					      unsigned long node)
> +static void __init fdt_init_reserved_mem_node(unsigned long node, const char *uname,
> +					      phys_addr_t base, phys_addr_t size)
>  {
>  	int err = 0;
>  	bool nomap;
>  
> +	struct reserved_mem *rmem = &reserved_mem[reserved_mem_count];
> +
> +	if (reserved_mem_count == total_reserved_mem_cnt) {
> +		pr_err("not enough space for all defined regions.\n");
> +		return;
> +	}

If we return early here, will we leak the physical memory? The caller has
already allocated or reserved the memory, so returning without calling
memblock_phys_free() or memblock_clear_nomap() might permanently lose this
memory.

> +
> +	rmem->name = uname;
> +	rmem->base = base;
> +	rmem->size = size;
> +
>  	nomap = of_get_flat_dt_prop(node, "no-map", NULL) != NULL;
>  
>  	err = __reserved_mem_init_node(rmem, node);
> @@ -614,6 +602,7 @@ static void __init fdt_init_reserved_mem_node(struct reserved_mem *rmem,
>  			memblock_clear_nomap(rmem->base, rmem->size);

Does this leak physical memory for dynamic nomap regions on initialization
failure? While this clears the MEMBLOCK_NOMAP flag, it doesn't free the
underlying memory. If the region was allocated dynamically, the physical
memory will remain permanently held in the memblock.reserved list.

>  		else
>  			memblock_phys_free(rmem->base, rmem->size);

Is it safe to unconditionally free the memory here? If a crafted or
misconfigured Device Tree defines a reserved region that overlaps critical
kernel memory (like kernel text or the initrd), memblock_reserve() blindly
accepts it. When initialization predictably fails, this unconditional
memblock_phys_free() could expose that critical memory to the page
allocator, potentially leading to memory corruption.

> +		return;

If we return early on error, the code skips incrementing reserved_mem_count,
meaning the next region will reuse this exact array slot.

Since we don't zero-initialize the slot before returning or before reusing it,
could this leak stale state? If the failed ops->node_init() callback modified
rmem->priv or other fields, those stale pointers will leak into the next
initialized region.
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Uninitialized state leakage due to `reserved_mem` array slot reuse.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"The patch changes the error handling behavior when `__reserved_mem_init_node()` fails. By returning early, `reserved_mem_count` is no longer incremented, meaning the next region will reuse the exact same `&reserved_mem[reserved_mem_count]` array slot. However, the code does not zero-initialize the slot before reusing it. If the failed `ops->node_init()` callback modified `rmem->priv` or other fields before returning an error, those stale pointers will leak into the next initialized region. If the subsequent region does not explicitly overwrite these fields, it will inherit garbage state, leading to potential Use-After-Free, kernel crashes, or undefined behavior when devices bind to the generic region."},{"problem":"Section attribute mismatch in `fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()` declaration.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"The patch adds a forward declaration for `fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()` without the `__init` section attribute, while the actual function definition is explicitly marked with `__init`. This section attribute mismatch violates kernel coding standards and will cause compiler warnings (such as section mismatch or missing prototype warnings) during the build."},{"problem":"Physical memory leak when `reserved_mem` array is full.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"In the new `fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()`, if `reserved_mem_count == total_reserved_mem_cnt` is true, the function logs an error and returns immediately. However, the physical memory for this region was already allocated or reserved by the caller (e.g., via `early_init_dt_alloc_reserved_memory_arch()` or `early_init_dt_reserve_memory()`). By returning early without calling `memblock_phys_free()` or `memblock_clear_nomap()`, the reserved memory is permanently leaked and lost to the page allocator."},{"problem":"Dynamic `nomap` regions leak physical memory on initialization failure.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"In the error handling path for failed specific initializations (`err != 0 && err != -ENODEV`), the code handles `nomap` regions by only calling `memblock_clear_nomap()`. While this successfully clears the `MEMBLOCK_NOMAP` flag, it does not free the underlying memory. For dynamic memory regions that were allocated via `memblock_phys_alloc_range()`, the physical memory remains permanently held in the `memblock.reserved` list. The error path must properly check and call `memblock_phys_free()` to prevent leaking physical memory for dynamic `nomap` regions."},{"problem":"Unconditional `memblock_phys_free()` allows unreserving critical kernel memory.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"If a region fails initialization, the error path unconditionally calls `memblock_phys_free()` for non-`nomap` regions. Because `memblock_reserve()` blindly accepts and merges overlapping regions without tracking ownership, a crafted or misconfigured Device Tree can define a reserved region that overlaps critical kernel memory (such as kernel text or the initrd). When the region predictably fails initialization, the unconditional `memblock_phys_free()` removes the critical memory from `memblock.reserved`, exposing it to the page allocator. This allows user-space to potentially allocate and overwrite kernel text, leading to severe memory corruption or privilege escalation."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 1e3f82ec25423c3e5a400a2068002129f04c7668
